did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a novel dual-branch attentional multi-fusion network (AMF-Net) combining EfficientNet-B3 and ResNet-50 with Convolutional Block Attention Modules (CBAM) for skin cancer classification. The model was trained on a dataset of 12,577 dermoscopic images to classify seven lesion classes, achieving 96.47% accuracy. It also employs Monte Carlo Dropout for uncertainty quantification and Grad-CAM++ for explainable AI visualizations.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work introducing a novel dual-branch attentional multi-fusion network (AMF-Net) for skin cancer classification. It details the development of the model, training on a curated dataset of 12,577 images, and reports specific experimental results including 96.47% accuracy, ROC-AUC scores, and uncertainty quantification via Monte Carlo Dropout. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICCIT 2025), it contains original research findings, ablation studies, and validation rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Dual-Branch Attentional Multi-Fusion Network with Uncertainty Quantification and Explainable AI for Skin Cancer Classification
journal: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
brief_summary: This study introduces AMF-Net, a dual-branch attentional multi-fusion network combining EfficientNet-B3 and ResNet-50 with Convolutional Block Attention Modules for skin cancer classification. The model was trained on a curated dataset of 12,577 dermoscopic images, merging the HAM10000, ISIC 2018, and a newly collected local hospital dataset. Evaluation on a stratified test set demonstrated state-of-the-art performance with 96.47% accuracy and macro-average ROC-AUC scores exceeding 0.90 across seven lesion classes. The framework incorporates Monte Carlo Dropout for uncertainty quantification and Grad-CAM++ for explainable AI visualizations validated by dermatologists. Results indicate that AMF-Net effectively balances high diagnostic accuracy with reliability, supporting a three-tier clinical triage system.

all_task_types.target: Seven discrete skin lesion classes (Melanocytic nevus, Melanoma, Benign keratosis, Basal cell carcinoma, Actinic keratosis, Vascular lesion, Dermatofibroma)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of dermoscopic images into seven specific diagnostic categories. The model predicts discrete lesion classes using a dual-branch attentional multi-fusion network (AMF-Net). Performance is evaluated using accuracy (96.47%), precision, recall, F1-score, confusion matrices, and ROC-AUC scores across the seven classes. Uncertainty quantification (Monte Carlo Dropout) is used to estimate confidence in these class predictions, not to predict a continuous outcome.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: ROC-AUC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve)
Uncertainty Quantification (Epistemic Uncertainty, Aleatoric Uncertainty, Total Uncertainty, Entropy)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To obtain 95% confidence intervals for performance metrics.
To quantify uncertainty (epistemic, aleatoric, and total) by conducting multiple stochastic forward passes for each image.
To divide the dataset into training (60%), validation (20%), and testing (20%) sets while preserving class proportions.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests for comparing models). It reported performance metrics (accuracy, AUC) and confidence intervals derived from bootstrap resampling, but did not conduct statistical hypothesis tests to compare AMF-Net against other models or baselines.

Combined Dataset (HAM10000 + ISIC 2018 +.notes: The model was trained on a merged dataset of 12,577 images (HAM10000: 10,015; ISIC 2018: 1,512; Local Hospital: 1,050) split 60/20/20 for training/validation/testing. The test set contained 2,516 images.

Combined Dataset (Test Set).notes: Higher epistemic uncertainty was observed in melanoma and actinic keratoses classes. On the test set, 72.3% of cases fell into the automated band (H < 0.5), 22.8% into expert-assisted, and 4.9% into expert-driven review.

Ablation Study.notes: The proposed dual-branch AMF-Net significantly exceeded all other variants in accuracy.

architecture_used.pretraining: EfficientNet-B3 and ResNet-50 backbones with a 60/40 freezing strategy to maintain general features while fine-tuning for skin lesion analysis.

architecture_details.attention_mechanism: Enhanced Convolutional Block Attention Modules (CBAM) applied to final feature maps of both branches, consisting of channel attention and spatial attention.
architecture_details.feature_extraction: Global average pooling reduces spatial dimensions; features projected into 1024-dimensional embedding space per branch.
architecture_details.fusion_method: Late fusion merging two 1024-dimensional vectors into a 2048-dimensional vector.
architecture_details.classifier_head: Deep Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) reducing dimensions from 2048 to 256, followed by Batch Normalization, ReLU activation, and Dropout (p=0.4). Final layer maps 256 dimensions to 7 logits.
architecture_details.regularization_in_backbone: Dropout with probability 0.4 in each branch's classification pipeline.

training_details.loss_function: Enhanced Focal Loss (gamma=3.0, alpha based on class weights) with label smoothing (epsilon=0.2). Class weights calculated to handle imbalance.
regularization_strategies: Data augmentation (flips, rotations, elastic deformations, blur, noise, color adjustments, CLAHE, CutMix, MixUp)

preprocessing_techniques: Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) to improve texture features
Blur and noise augmentations (Gaussian and motion blur)
Color-based augmentations (random adjustments to brightness, contrast, and hue-saturation-value)
Regularization strategies simulating occlusions (coarse dropout, grid dropout)
Stratified split of dataset into training (60%), validation (20%), and testing (20%) sets
Calculation of class weights to handle class imbalance

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Random adjustments to brightness, contrast, and hue-saturation-value.
Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) to improve texture features.
Coarse dropout and grid dropout to simulate occlusions and enhance generalization.
CutMix and MixUp with lambda parameter sampled from Beta(1.0, 0.2).

conclusions: The proposed AMF-Net achieved state-of-the-art performance with 96.47% overall accuracy and macro-average ROC-AUC scores exceeding 0.90 across seven skin lesion classes on a combined dataset of 12,577 images.

The model demonstrated perfect classification (100% accuracy and AUC) for basal cell carcinoma and dermatofibroma, and strong performance (precision and recall >95%) for melanoma, benign keratosis, and vascular lesions.
Actinic keratoses were identified as the most challenging class, with the lowest AUC (0.933) and lower precision (0.824) compared to other classes.

Calibrated uncertainty quantification via Monte Carlo Dropout enabled a three-tier clinical triage system (automated, expert-assisted, expert-driven), with 72.3% of cases falling into the automated band (uncertainty < 0.5) and higher epistemic uncertainty observed for melanoma and actinic keratoses.
Grad-CAM++ explainable AI visualizations highlighted clinically relevant features and were validated by dermatologists, improving interpretability and trust in the model's predictions.

An ablation study confirmed the superiority of the dual-branch attentional multi-fusion design, with AMF-Net significantly outperforming single-backbone models (e.g., ResNet-50 at 76.21%, EfficientNet-B3 at 77.96%) and variants without attention or dropout.
The inclusion of a newly collected local hospital dataset alongside public benchmarks (HAM10000, ISIC 2018) enhanced clinical applicability and dataset diversity beyond standard public benchmarks.
The study acknowledges the need for future large-scale external validation across multiple institutions and optimization for deployment in real-world healthcare settings.
important_remarks: The study merges public datasets (HAM10000, ISIC 2018) with a small local hospital dataset (1,050 images) collected via smartphone; this introduces potential bias regarding image quality, lighting conditions, and demographic representation compared to standardized clinical dermoscopy.

The paper acknowledges the need for future large-scale external validation across multiple institutions, indicating that current results may not generalize to diverse clinical settings without further testing.
Actinic keratoses (akiec) were identified as the most challenging class with the lowest AUC (0.933) and lower precision (0.824), suggesting potential reliability issues for this specific diagnosis.
Basal cell carcinoma (bcc) and dermatofibroma (df) achieved 100% accuracy and AUC, which may indicate class dominance or lack of difficulty in the test set rather than perfect model capability.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was conducted to compare AMF-Net against baselines; significance is inferred from performance metrics and ablation studies.

The model relies on Monte Carlo Dropout for uncertainty quantification, which increases inference time due to multiple stochastic forward passes, potentially impacting real-time deployment efficiency.
The inclusion of a web-based application demonstrates deployment intent, but no real-world clinical workflow integration or user study results are reported.
